storytellers.bio: Joe Kwon is a compassionate innovator and leader, committed to supporting those who drive positive change in communities. His work revolves around creating spaces for leaders to thrive, inspired by his connections with figures like Benjamin Knight and Chelsea Gill. Joe's dedication shines through in projects like Confi, an initiative aimed at empowering leaders by addressing vulnerability and fostering resilience. Known for his collaborative spirit, he bridges gaps between ideas and execution, tirelessly working to uplift the communities he serves.

stories.title: Gaith's Story
stories.content:
I'm now five and a half months out of detention and working at the Confit gym, living what I consider the dream. 

My name’s Gaith, I'm 19 years old and proud of my Iraqi Muslim roots. 

My early years were in Roselands & Lakemba, and for a time, I went back to Iraq. Coming back, I started making bad choices, falling in with the wrong crowd, which eventually got me locked up.

That time in detention? It turned out to be a hidden blessing. It forced me to take a hard look at my life and the decisions I was making. Inside, I learned the importance of appreciating the small things - like waking up, having a meal, or just being thankful for the people in my life. It was tough, missing out on life outside, feeling like the world was moving on without me. But it was also a wake-up call, showing me there's help and hope beyond the walls of detention.

I first connected with the Confit program while inside, and it was different from anything else. The mentors, they got it. They'd been where I was and didn't just talk the talk; they walked it with us. That authenticity made all the difference. They showed me through their own stories and dedication that change was possible.

When I got out, all the plans I had made and the promises I'd been given by others fell through. I felt stuck. Remembering the Confit folks, I called Andy, and just like that, I had a new direction. They were just starting to build the gym, and I got to be a part of that from the ground up.

Now, my life is completely different. Before, I was always out causing trouble, stressing out my family. These days, I have a routine that includes training, praying, and eating right. I've found structure, and with that, peace. It's amazing how much can change when you find the right support.

To anyone who's struggling like I was, know that it's never too late to turn things around. Reach out, take that chance on change, and maybe, like me, you'll end up living a dream you never thought possible.

stories.title: Dani — Confit Pathways
stories.content: Dani is part of the Confit Pathways program.

stories.title: Tina McPhee - Story's Story
stories.content:
The Unspoken Narratives and Their Impact: An Odyssey Begins

In the intricate weave of the criminal justice system, a hidden odyssey emerges: the profound tales of those who've journeyed through its depths. Tina McPhee's experience, mirroring a hero's quest, unravels these often-silenced narratives, bringing to light the resilience and transformation that exist beneath the layers of societal preconceptions. Her transition from a prisoner to an advocate is not merely a change in status but an epic evolution, challenging and reshaping our perceptions of justice and reintegration.

"I never like to feed into that narrative of, you know, some people who have a violent offence, or some people who spend a little time in prison, as it creates a binary even amongst people that have been in prison." - Tina McPhee

In revealing these stories, Tina does more than share her own tale; she beckons us to embark on a transformative journey alongside her. This journey is a call to dismantle the barriers of perception that so often cloud our understanding of incarceration. Tina urges us to move beyond the confines of statistics and policy discussions, to a place where we can appreciate the rich, human essence of each individual's story. She challenges us to see the faces, hear the voices, and feel the emotions of those who have traversed the path she knows all too well.

Her narrative is a beacon, guiding us through a maze of misunderstandings and stereotypes. It's an invitation to explore the uncharted territories of human experience within the criminal justice system. By sharing her story, Tina not only provides a window into her soul but also holds up a mirror for society to reflect on its own perceptions and prejudices.

As she shares her journey, Tina brings a nuanced understanding of the complexities faced by those in prison. She reveals how the experience of incarceration isn't uniform but is instead filled with a spectrum of stories and backgrounds, each carrying its weight and significance. Her words serve as a reminder that behind every statistic, policy debate, or headline, there's a human story waiting to be heard and understood. This understanding is crucial for meaningful reform and for bridging the gap between society and those who return to it from the system.

Trauma and Its Lasting Imprint: The Dragon in Disguise

Tina McPhee's narrative unfolds a tale where incarceration stands not as an isolated event, but as the climactic confrontation in a journey marred by personal trauma and systemic failures. This part of her odyssey is akin to facing a dragon, not in the physical sense, but as a metaphorical battle against the deep-seated shadows of past traumas that have long shaped her destiny. Her story resonates with a universal struggle, where the real adversary is often the unseen scars and the unresolved anguish from one's past.

"Each time I faced the consequences of my actions, I realised they were not just about the moment of crime but about a lifetime of hurt and confusion that I carried within me." - Tina McPhee

Tina's introspection into her past unveils a truth often neglected in discussions about criminal justice: the profound impact of personal trauma on an individual's journey to incarceration. Her reflections highlight that many who find themselves within the justice system are often battling internal dragons, wrought from years of pain, neglect, or abuse. Tina's story challenges the simplistic notion of crime and punishment, urging us to consider the complex tapestry of experiences that lead individuals down certain paths.

This narrative shift calls for a reimagining of the justice system. Tina advocates for a transformation where the system becomes a place of healing and understanding, rather than solely a tool for punishment. She envisions a justice system that not only addresses the actions leading to incarceration but also delves into the deeper, underlying issues that individuals carry with them. It's a call for a journey from darkness to enlightenment, where the focus shifts from retribution to rehabilitation, from punishment to healing.

Through Tina's eyes, we see the need for a holistic approach to justice, one that considers the entire spectrum of an individual's life experiences. Her story serves as a powerful reminder that effective rehabilitation and reintegration require us to confront and address the root causes of behavior, not just the behavior itself. In doing so, we not only aid in the healing process of those incarcerated but also contribute to the creation of a more compassionate and just society.

Tina's confrontation with her past traumas and her journey towards healing and advocacy symbolize a crucial step in transforming how society views and handles crime and rehabilitation. Her story is a testament to the power of confronting our inner dragons and emerging with newfound strength and purpose, a journey that has the potential to inspire systemic change and bring about a new era of justice and understanding.

The Culture of Secret Keeping and Its Perils: Navigating the Maze

In Tina McPhee's journey, the act of secret-keeping emerges as a labyrinthine maze, fraught with hidden dangers and deceptive turns. This aspect of her story reveals a critical facet of her life and the lives of many who find themselves entangled in the justice system. It's a tale of how the silence born from fear, shame, and societal stigma can become chains that bind one’s true self, preventing healing and growth.

"The more we keep secrets, the more we feed the cycle of shame and isolation. Breaking that cycle is key to healing, not just for the individual but for society as a whole." - Tina McPhee

Tina's passage through this maze of secrets is emblematic of a broader societal issue. She unveils how the culture of secret-keeping, often a survival mechanism, perpetuates a vicious cycle that deepens the wounds of trauma and alienation. Her story is not just about the secrets she kept but also about the societal norms that compel individuals to hide parts of themselves, to live in the shadows out of fear of judgment or rejection.

Her struggle and eventual breakthrough in speaking her truth highlight the transformative power of open dialogue. Tina's advocacy for transparency and honesty becomes a guiding light for others. It's an invitation to journey through their own mazes of secrecy, to confront and dismantle the barriers that silence them. This openness not only liberates the individual but also fosters a collective understanding and empathy, essential for societal healing.

Navigating this maze, Tina discovers and shares a profound truth: the act of revealing one's story is not just about personal catharsis but also about breaking down the walls of misunderstanding and prejudice that divide us. Her call for open dialogue is a call for a cultural shift, where honesty is not met with judgment but with compassion and support.

In this crucial chapter of her narrative, Tina emerges as a navigator, a mentor who has traversed the treacherous paths of secret-keeping and emerged with wisdom to share. Her story becomes a beacon for those re-entering society, offering hope and a roadmap for finding one's voice amidst the noise of societal expectations and personal fears. By sharing her journey, Tina illuminates the path for others, showing that the maze of secret-keeping, while complex and daunting, can be navigated and overcome. Her journey through this maze becomes a pivotal part of her hero's journey, underscoring the importance of truth and transparency in the quest for justice, healing, and reintegration.

Personal Transformation and Ongoing Challenges: The Phoenix Rises

As Tina McPhee steps out of the shadows of incarceration, her journey takes on the metaphorical semblance of a phoenix rising from the ashes. This phase of her life story is not just about liberation from physical confinement; it is about the transformative journey that follows, marked by resilience, constant vigilance, and the daunting challenge of reintegration into society.

"I tend to manage anxiety and stress through compulsive behaviour. So, I get very addicted to things, particularly routines and processes, very easily." - Tina McPhee

Tina’s words echo the intricate struggles that define the lives of those re-entering society. The process is akin to navigating a new world, where the past remains a relentless shadow and the future is a canvas of uncertainty. Her candid admission about managing anxiety and stress underscores a reality often overlooked: the psychological battles don't end upon release from prison; they evolve into new forms, demanding continuous effort and adaptation.

Her journey is a poignant reminder of the myriad challenges that individuals face upon re-entry. The routine and structure of prison life, once a source of constraint, can leave a void that is hard to fill in the outside world. This transition requires not just a physical but a psychological and emotional recalibration, a process where old habits and coping mechanisms may resurface, and new strategies for resilience must be forged.

Tina’s narrative symbolises the transformative power of personal growth and the importance of support systems in this journey. Her experience points to the need for robust re-entry programs that do more than just address basic needs for employment and housing. There is a crucial need for holistic support systems that consider mental health, emotional well-being, and social reintegration.

As she navigates these challenges, Tina becomes more than a survivor; she emerges as an emblem of hope and renewal. Her story is a testament to the human capacity to adapt, grow, and redefine one’s path even in the face of relentless challenges. It is a narrative that resonates with many who find themselves on a similar path, searching for ways to rebuild their lives and identity in a world that has changed in their absence.

Through her ongoing journey of self-discovery and adaptation, Tina illustrates that the path to reintegration is not linear but is filled with obstacles, learning experiences, and opportunities for growth. Her story serves as an inspiration, showing that while the journey is fraught with challenges, it also holds the potential for remarkable personal transformation, much like the mythical phoenix rising from its ashes to begin anew.

Forging Bonds in Unlikely Places: The Fellowship Within

In the unexpected crucible of prison, Tina McPhee discovered an unlikely yet profound fellowship. It’s a narrative thread that weaves through her story, revealing the often-overlooked human connections that form in the most challenging environments. Her experiences in prison highlight a powerful truth: even in the midst of hardship and isolation, the human spirit seeks and often finds companionship, solidarity, and understanding.

"You do create bonds with people that I will never forget. I am still friends with numerous women who I spent time with in prison and who have been with me for a number of years, whom I really enjoy the company of." - Tina McPhee

Tina's reflection on these bonds sheds light on a crucial aspect of the human condition: our intrinsic need for connection and community. Her story illustrates that the dark corridors of a prison can, paradoxically, become spaces where meaningful, life-changing relationships are forged. These connections, formed under the most trying circumstances, speak to the resilience of the human heart and the capacity to find hope and camaraderie where one might least expect it.

These relationships, built on shared experiences and mutual understanding, become more than just friendships; they transform into a vital support network. For Tina and many others, these bonds have played an essential role in navigating the complexities of prison life and the challenges of re-entry into society. They offer a sense of belonging, a reminder that one is not alone in their journey, and a source of strength and encouragement during difficult times.

Furthermore, Tina’s story of forging bonds in prison becomes emblematic of the broader journey towards healing and reintegration. It underscores the importance of building and maintaining relationships as a crucial element of rehabilitation. The support and understanding derived from these connections are invaluable for those re-entering society, helping to ease the transition and reduce the sense of alienation that can accompany release.

In essence, Tina's experiences reveal that within the confines of prison, a profound fellowship can emerge, one that challenges our preconceptions of these environments. Her narrative invites us to reconsider the power of human connections and their transformative impact, not only within the prison walls but also as a fundamental part of the journey towards healing and reintegration. These bonds, forged in unlikely places, become beacons of hope and solidarity, illuminating the path for those navigating the intricate process of returning to society.

Facing Cruelty and Indifference: The Battle for Empathy

In her harrowing journey, Tina McPhee encounters a formidable adversary: the pervasive cruelty and indifference within the prison system. This chapter of her story casts her in the role of a warrior, not armed with weapons but with the power of her voice and experience, advocating fiercely for empathy and systemic reform. Her battle is fought not just within the confines of prison walls but in the broader arena of societal attitudes and policies.

"The hardest part to wrap my head around was the complete cruelty in terms of staff... that same kind of violence and aggression, and really disparaging sexist remarks towards women from both male and female staff in corrections." - Tina McPhee

Tina’s reflections on the treatment she and others received from correctional staff reveal a grim reality of the prison environment. Her accounts of violence, aggression, and sexist remarks are stark reminders of the inhumanity that can pervade such institutions. These experiences are not just isolated incidents but symptomatic of deeper systemic issues that extend beyond individual facilities.

In standing up against this cruelty and indifference, Tina’s narrative transforms into a larger battle for empathy and human dignity. She challenges the very foundations of the prison system, which often prioritizes punishment over rehabilitation, and neglects the basic human rights and respect owed to every individual, regardless of their circumstances.

Tina's fight extends beyond personal grievances; it becomes a crusade against the societal perceptions and policies that contribute to the dehumanization of inmates. She calls for a shift in how society views those in prison, advocating for a perspective that recognizes their humanity, struggles, and potential for rehabilitation. Her story is a powerful indictment of the prevailing punitive approach and a plea for a more compassionate, empathetic response to criminal justice.

Moreover, Tina's advocacy serves as a beacon for others who have endured similar experiences. Her courage in speaking out offers hope and validation to those who have felt voiceless and marginalized. It also serves as a rallying cry for systemic change, urging policymakers, correctional authorities, and the broader public to reevaluate and reform their approaches to incarceration.

In essence, Tina McPhee’s battle for empathy in the face of cruelty and indifference is a poignant reminder of the urgent need for reform in the criminal justice system. Her journey becomes a testament to the power of resilience, advocacy, and the human spirit in confronting and overcoming the challenges posed by an often indifferent system. Through her story, Tina emerges not just as a survivor of injustice but as a catalyst for change, inspiring others to join her in the fight for a more empathetic, just, and humane approach to incarceration.

The Dance of Visibility and Anonymity: The Hero's Dilemma

In the narrative of Tina McPhee’s life, post-incarceration presents a unique and complex challenge, emblematic of the hero’s dilemma. It is a delicate dance between embracing her identity as a person with lived experience for the purpose of advocacy and yearning for the cloak of anonymity. This struggle between visibility and obscurity symbolizes a broader conflict faced by many upon re-entering society, where the desire to contribute meaningfully to societal discourse is often counterbalanced by the need for personal privacy and normalcy.

"Sometimes you're wanting to be seen as someone with lived experience... and then there's that other side of the line where I just don't want to be seen; I would like to be unseen." - Tina McPhee

Tina’s words echo the sentiments of numerous individuals who, upon leaving the confines of prison, grapple with their public persona. They must navigate how much of their past to reveal and how much to keep private, a decision that can impact their social interactions, employment opportunities, and personal well-being. Tina’s experience highlights the nuanced nature of this journey, where being a spokesperson or advocate for change comes with its own set of challenges and vulnerabilities.

Her narrative offers invaluable insight into the complexities of reintegration and the pursuit of advocacy. It’s a journey that requires courage to confront societal stigmas, the wisdom to know when to share and when to withhold, and the strength to stand in one’s truth while also protecting one’s personal space. Tina's story serves as a guiding light for others in similar situations, offering a roadmap for balancing the personal and the public, the seen and the unseen.

In this dance of visibility and anonymity, Tina McPhee emerges as more than just a survivor or an advocate; she becomes a symbol of the intricate dance of identity that many returning citizens must perform. Her journey through this dance is not just about her own story but is reflective of a larger narrative experienced by those re-entering society after incarceration. It’s a narrative that speaks to the heart of human identity, dignity, and the ongoing quest for a place in society post-incarceration.

Through her experiences, Tina illuminates the path for others facing this dilemma, showing that it is possible to navigate these intricate dynamics and find a balance that respects both the need for personal privacy and the desire to make a meaningful impact. Her story becomes a beacon for understanding and empathy, highlighting the importance of supporting those who re-enter society in their endeavor to find their place and their voice in a world that has often moved on without them.

Conclusion: The Hero's Legacy and A Call to Action

As the curtain falls on the epic narrative of Tina McPhee's journey through the labyrinth of the criminal justice system, it becomes evident that her path has been nothing short of a hero's journey. Faced with trials that tested her resilience, she underwent profound transformations and emerged with triumphs not just for herself, but as a beacon of hope and change for society. Her story transcends the personal; it becomes a powerful clarion call urging us to rethink and reshape our societal approach to justice, rehabilitation, and reintegration.

Tina’s odyssey is a poignant reminder of the complexities and struggles that individuals face when they intersect with the justice system and the challenges that continue as they re-enter society. Her narrative goes beyond recounting experiences; it serves as a compelling call to action. It implores us to listen attentively, learn empathetically, and act decisively to transform the way we perceive and engage with those who have encountered the justice system.

But more than that, Tina’s story is an invitation. It invites us to join her on this transformative journey – to be part of a movement that fosters a more empathetic, understanding, and effective system. Her experiences, insights, and lessons are invaluable resources that can guide us in building a society that not only rehabilitates but also integrates, supports, and uplifts.

As we absorb the essence of Tina's story, we are called upon to contribute to a legacy that goes beyond her individual journey. It’s a legacy that can shape a future where the criminal justice system is seen not as a terminal point but as a crossroad that offers opportunities for growth, redemption, and positive societal contribution.

In the end, Tina McPhee's narrative is more than just her story. It's a catalyst for change, a testament to the power of resilience, and an enduring reminder of the impact we can make when we view each person's journey through a lens of compassion and understanding. Her story concludes, but the ripple effects of her journey continue, inspiring us to take up the mantle and be part of the ongoing quest for a just, empathetic, and rehabilitative society.

stories.title: Knox / Confit Mentee's Story
stories.content:
Stealing began with little things, like chocolates from a store. It was a way to feel in charge of my life that was getting messy. But being around others doing the same, it got worse. Bad decisions came easy.

When my folks split up, it hit hard. Home wasn't home anymore. I started hanging out with the wrong crowd, trying to escape what was happening at home. Each small theft felt like moving away from my problems, but I was actually getting deeper into trouble.

Getting caught and locked up, I thought it was all over. But it turned out to be a good thing. If that hadn't happened, I might have gone down a really bad road. Being inside made me think about where I was heading. I knew I had to change, not just for me, but for those I care about.

In juvi, I met the fullas from ConFit Pathways They didn't just see a bad kid; they saw someone who could do better. They showed me there’s another way to live. It wasn’t just about stopping the bad stuff, but about changing how I think and see the world.

Now, I’ve got plans, simple but big ones for me. Like getting a car and saving for a house. It’s not just about things; it’s about setting up a good future. I want to show that change can happen, that we're not only our mistakes. I started off wrong, but now I’m heading in a good direction. I want a life I can be proud of, more than just the bad choices I made.

By telling my story, I hope others see that lots of us get pushed down tough paths. But with a bit of help and understanding, anyone can turn things around. Everyone should have a chance to make their story better."

stories.title: Metan Bacir Kapula / Confit Mentee's Story
stories.content:
Who am I? Just a young and dumb kid, trying to make money for the family. I took the fast route instead of the slow one. That's who I am. 

My name is Metan Bacir Kapula. They call me the Turkish Stallion, that's better, you know. Whoever shows me respect gets it back, with more love. That's my way. Just don't cross my wrong path.

I've been out of juvenile detention for about five or six months now. Finding a job with Confit was a game changer for me. One of the boys from Juvi introduced me this gig, and since then, I've been working hard and feeling grateful.

I come from a Turkish family and we're a pretty normal, middle Eastern style strict but full of love. Everything's for family and that's why, as a man, you try to do the providing thing leading to trouble with the law. But now, with Joe and the boys, I'm getting better.

I got into trouble trying to make money for the family. We came from a broken house and I felt like I had to do my part, provide something to help. I did 10 months inside, got out for 10 days, and was back in for another 10 months. They dropped the charges because I didn't do anything. Just allegations, but they made me the better man I am today.

When Joe and the lads came into detention, it was like we could all relate. We've been through the same stuff, so it's good to have someone to talk to about it. Here at the gym it’s all about working and having fun at the same time, it's more than just a job. It's like a brotherhood, a friendship.

If you're getting out of detention, believe me when I say, these guys are brothers. They really looked after us, and I'm appreciative to Confit. 

What's next for me? Inshallah, I'll start boxing again. That's been my dream since I was young and I want to stay out of trouble. My dream is probably to retire on an island in Turkey, nothing too much. Just casual, you know. Horses, quiet existence and a small house. 

Tips on life? Look after yourself. To look after the people you love, you've gotta look after yourself. Stay healthy and strong-minded.

To Joe and the boys, just thank you. I appreciate it, straight out. That's it.

Reflections

Metan's story is a visceral reminder of the complexities that young people face when growing up in challenging environments. His journey from the streets to striving for a better life is a powerful narrative of resilience and redemption. It's a candid look into the realities that many young people face, and the crucial role that support networks play in their rehabilitation and growth.

His focus on family, the struggle for respect, and the pursuit of dreams reflect a universal human experience, yet underscored by the unique challenges of his background and circumstances. Metan's determination to turn his life around, his gratitude for the support he received, and his simple yet profound life philosophy offer hope and inspiration.

His story, raw and unfiltered, speaks volumes about the potential for change and the power of having the right support at the right time. It's a testament to the strength of the human spirit and the unyielding desire to pursue a better, more fulfilling life.

stories.title: Moana — Confit Pathways
stories.content: Moana is part of the Confit Pathways program.

stories.title: Hamilton's Story
stories.content:
"I was actually signed to the Rabbitohs when I got locked up for armed robbery. It ended up being three years inside...but like

Joe Kwon

always says it was a part of my life and I can now use that to create an impact for others."

The sergeant at the police station said he had to make a phone call to my Mum. I was heartbroken and I shitting myself. The last thing you wanna do is let your parents find out. And then when my Nan jumped the phone, she couldn't talk, she was lost for words and that absolutely broke my heart.

I remember the first day they came and they visited. As much as I wanted them to come...just seeing the look on their faces when they walked in...it was hard to control the emotion with tears just pouring out of their eyes out.

That moment changed my mind forever. I have so much love for my grandmother and putting my family through what they had to go through was a pivotal reminder of what I needed to do. More than the punishment - this moment was the push I needed to change.

You can always see the potential in the boys. You look at a few and they need a little bit more work but we always have hope for them. Getting out and going back into the same environment is just straight hard, some of them have very little choices.

We tell the boys...at the end of the day it's really up to you. If you want to make a change in your life, to get out of the environment and choose a different path...we can be a part of that. We provide them with informed pathways, education or whatever they want to do in life too."

The raw emotion and stark reality of Hamilton's experiences serves as an important reminder of the collective responsibility we hold. It's our duty to extend a hand, to offer pathways that lead away from the precipice of reoffending and towards a horizon of possibilities. The

ConFit Pathways

program embodies this mission, providing a compass for young souls navigating the tumultuous seas of the youth justice system.

Hamilton is one of my heroes and I want to share more of these stories. Sometimes I am not sure of posting these on something like LinkedIn, but if it sparks just ones persons interest in helping out a young person with a criminal history, it will be worth it.

More

ConFit Pathways

stories coming soon.

A Curious Tractor

is working on a storytelling platform to celebrate the amazing work happening in the community to drive change and build across all areas of the youth justice system.

stories.title: Joe — Confit Pathways
stories.content: Joe is part of the Confit Pathways program.

stories.title: Eddie / Confit Mentee's Story
stories.content:
Looking back, my life's been a bit of a rollercoaster. Growing up in Mount Druitt was tough. We had fun, sure, but it wasn't easy. Being in a gang felt like belonging to something, even if it meant getting into trouble. The day I got arrested, it hit me hard. I realized then I'd made some bad choices.

In jail, life slowed down. It was tough, but it gave me time to think about where I went wrong. Lifting weights and getting stronger was one thing I could control. It helped me feel a bit better about myself.

Getting out of jail was scary and exciting at the same time. Life outside had moved on without me, and I had to figure out how to fit back in. Joe and the guys from the concrete job really helped me out. They didn't just give me work; they gave me a chance to start over.

Looking at everything, I've learned a lot. My past mistakes don't define me. What matters is how I move forward. I've had my ups and downs, but I'm working on making things better. That's what my story's all about – getting a second chance and trying to do better with it.

stories.title: Vireak / Confit Mentor's Story
stories.content:
Growing up in Cabramatta, I was surrounded by a life that many can't even imagine. If you do the research on back in the eighties and nineties it was rooted with a lot of heroin, a lot of crime, a lot of gangs. This environment, it shaped me, but it also trapped me.

My descent into crime wasn't something I'm proud of, but it felt like the only way to survive at the time. I from women's refugee to women's refugee with Mum being a prolific drug dealer back in those days. I probably started using Heroin when I was 14 and as it was all around me and a way to move past some of my dark feelings. It was a hard life, filled with constant challenges and few escapes. As I grew older I started to find ways to support my habits, so I started selling drugs. 

The struggle with addiction is hard to describe unless you've been through it. I felt like I was constantly going round in circles, feeling like there might be a chance to stop but the tug of the drugs being too strong. But what I tell the boys is, don't stop trying to stop. There's bad habits but when you give up that's when it consumes you. That was my reality, a cycle of addiction that seemed never-ending.

My time in youth detention and prison was a harsh reality check. It was there I faced the consequences of my actions head-on. The cold bars and the isolation from the outside world made me reflect on my life choices. It's a part of my life that I don't look back on fondly, but it was a crucial step in my journey towards change. The days and nights I spent there were filled with introspection and a growing resolve to turn my life around. Its funny though, I don’t think the punishment was the thing that turned me around, it was getting over this lifestyle of drug addiction and constantly chasing that forced my hand. I was just over it!

I chose to make a change...in my head I was just set for failure. From my teachers, to my family, to my community, the only think I saw, heard and felt was that Vic is loser. I believed that for a long time but in changing my mindset came changing my health, wellbeing and a new life.

Talking about my family, especially my relationship with my mom and brother…it's complex. My mom, who was involved in running drugs, and I, we've come a long way. We've learned to rebuild our lives, leaving behind the crime and drugs. My brother, however, is a different story. My brother's still in jail. He's been in and out of jail. So I use my brother as part of the stories that I tell the boys, because I chose to make a change and he just kept on doing the same thing over and over again. It's tough to talk about, but it's a reality that shapes my message to the kids I mentor.

It is really tough to tell this story. But, when I share my story, I see the impact it has. My story is going to impact a lot more lives than it would just burning it inside me. People will always judge you, no matter what and that will continue to be tough tough, but trying. Sharing my journey, it's not just about me. It's about helping others see that change is possible, no matter where you come from.

stories.title: Mount Isa Trip — Confit Pathways
stories.content: Mount Isa Trip is part of the Confit Pathways program.
